coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent platform based on xylan-derived carbon dots for detecting Vibrio parahaemolyticus
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Xiwen
Yu, Shanshan
Cai, Jihai
Wang, Xiaoying
coredata.dc:description: It is a challenge to design a ratiometric fluorescent platform with a stable inner reference free from disturbing for detection. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent platform (RFP) was fabricated for detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus (V. parahaemolyticus) based on optically transparent silica nanoparticles which integrates fluorophores and specific aptamers. The platform incorporated Rhodamine B as an internal reference within the protective silica matrix, while xylan-derived carbon dots were evenly distributed on silica’s surface. When excited at 365 nm, RFP exhibited blue fluorescence at 430 nm and orange fluorescence at 575 nm. Blue fluorescence of these carbon dots was quenched through interaction with a Dabcyl-modified aptamer (Apt.D). Upon bacterial addition, changes in the fluorescence intensities were observed, enabling sensitive and ratiometric detection. Notably, this RFP-Apt.D has a low detection limit of 1.15 CFU/mL (S/N = 3) with a linear detection range from 8 CFU/mL to 8 ×105 CFU/mL in a short responding time of 30 minutes, which was superior to most reported V. parahaemolyticus sensors. Furthermore, the RFP-Apt.D was successfully applied to detect V. parahaemolyticus in seawater and clam samples through a fast pretreatment without requiring enrichment and achieved reliable and satisfactory recoveries. This work provides a widely adaptable platform to design ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detection of foodborne pathogen.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensing platform
Ratiometric fluorescence
Vibrio parahaemolyticus
Xylan
High sensitivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of xylan derived carbon dots with blue fluorescence (BCDs)
Preparation of ammoniated RhB doped silica (RhB@SiO2)
Preparation of ratiometric fluorescence platform with Dabcyl-modified aptamer (RFP-Apt.D)
Cultivation and detection of V. parahaemolyticus
Measurement of foodborne pathogens in real samples
Characterization of SiO2, RhB@SiO2, RFP, RFP-Apt.D
Sensitivity, selectivity and stability of RFP-Apt.D

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of SiO2, RhB@SiO2, BCDs, RFP and RFP-Apt.D: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra, (b) FT-IR spectra, (c) size distribution, (d) Zeta potential, (e) XPS spectra and (f) high-resolution XPS spectra of P element for RFP-Apt.D.

The SEM image (a) and TEM image (b) of RhB@SiO2. The TEM images of BCDs (c, d), RFP (f, g, h) with its EDS images (i). (e) diagram about synthesis of proportional fluorescence platform. The SEM image (j) and TEM image (k) of RFP-Apt.D with its EDS images (l, m).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of SiO2, RhB, BCDs, RhB@SiO2, RFP, and RFP-Apt.D under excitation of 365 nm; (b) Fluorescence spectra of RFP; (c) CIE chromaticity coordinates of BCDs, RhB@SiO2, RFP, and RFP-Apt.D, the inset were images of RhB@SiO2, BCDs, RFP and RFP-Apt.D under natural light (upper layer, from left to right) and 365 nm UV (lower layer); (d) The fluorescence excitation spectrum of RhB@SiO2, BCDs, RFP.

(a) Schematic diagram of bacterial recognition mechanism of RFP-Apt.D; (b) Fluorescence spectra of RFP-Apt.D with V. parahaemolyticus of different concentrations; (c) Calibration curves of ratiometric fluorescent intensity vs. the concentration of V. parahaemolyticus; (d) Fluorescence spectra of RFP-Apt.D with other bacteria; (e) The corresponding ratiometric fluorescent response; (f) Fluorescent stability of RFP-Apt.D. (g) Comparison of fluorescent sensors for V. parahaemolyticus detection.

(a) Plate cultivation of V. parahaemolyticus in seawater and clams. The fluorescence spectra and the calibration curves of ratiometric fluorescent intensity vs. the V. parahaemolyticus’s concentration of RFP-Apt.D with V. parahaemolyticus in real samples of seawater (b, c) and clams (d, e).
The fluorescence lifetime of RFP, RFP-Apt.D, and RFP-Apt.D-V. parahaemolyticus.
Comparison of fluorescent sensors for V. parahaemolyticus detection.

row.entry: Magnetic and up-conversion nanoparticles combined with aptamers

Magnetic capture particles (CPs), and europium-fluorescent
labeling particles (LPs)
functionalized with antibodies
Immunomagnetic nanoparticles and immunofluorescence QD probes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery for the determination of V. parahaemolyticus in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent platform based on xylan-derived carbon dots for detecting Vibrio parahaemolyticus

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Wushan Road, Tianhe District, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Wushan Road, Tianhe District, Guangzhou 510640, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is a challenge to design a ratiometric fluorescent platform with a stable inner reference free from disturbing for detection. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent platform (RFP) was fabricated for detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus (V. parahaemolyticus) based on optically transparent silica nanoparticles which integrates fluorophores and specific aptamers. The platform incorporated Rhodamine B as an internal reference within the protective silica matrix, while xylan-derived carbon dots were evenly distributed on silica’s surface. When excited at 365 nm, RFP exhibited blue fluorescence at 430 nm and orange fluorescence at 575 nm. Blue fluorescence of these carbon dots was quenched through interaction with a Dabcyl-modified aptamer (Apt.D). Upon bacterial addition, changes in the fluorescence intensities were observed, enabling sensitive and ratiometric detection. Notably, this RFP-Apt.D has a low detection limit of 1.15 CFU/mL (S/N = 3) with a linear detection range from 8 CFU/mL to 8 ×105 CFU/mL in a short responding time of 30 minutes, which was superior to most reported V. parahaemolyticus sensors. Furthermore, the RFP-Apt.D was successfully applied to detect V. parahaemolyticus in seawater and clam samples through a fast pretreatment without requiring enrichment and achieved reliable and satisfactory recoveries. This work provides a widely adaptable platform to design ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detection of foodborne pathogen.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vibrio parahaemolyticus (V. parahaemolyticus) is a common foodborne enteropathogen in seafood, especially clams, leading to a series of gastrointestinal disorders (Baker-Austin et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2019). Clinical symptoms include watery diarrhea, abdominal cramps, nausea, vomiting, headache, and fever (Baker-Austin et al., 2018; Elmahdi et al., 2016). Moreover, V. parahaemolyticus can invade the bloodstream through open wounds , can even cause septicemia, posing a severe threat to life in critical conditions (Baker-Austin et al., 2018; Elmahdi et al., 2016). Therefore, it is paramount to develop biosensors with highsensitivity, highselectivity and fast-response for the detection of V. parahaemolyticus (Sadanandan et al., 2023; Zheng et al., 2023).

Over the years, significant efforts have been dedicated to developing detection technology like culture-based methods, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) (Das et al., 2021; Thao Nguyen et al., 2020). These methods above have the disadvantages of labour intensity, time consumption, and specialized equipment requirements, which limits their real-world applications in sample testing (Dang et al., 2025). Remarkably, biosensors based on aptamers and fluorescence show high specific binding affinity and rapid response in very few steps. Dang et al. developed a 6-carboxyfluorescein (FAM)-labeled S. typhimurium aptamers (Sal-Apt) adsorbed graphitic carbon nitride (GCNs) for Salmonella Typhimurium detection with a limit of detection values as low as 8 and 3 CFU/mL when using colorimetric and fluorometric methods, respectively (Dang et al., 2025). Wang et al. reported a probe of Zr&Eu (2, 6-NDC) and FAM-aptamer for V. parahaemolyticus rapid analysis (Wang et al., 2023). Ren et al. proposed aptamer-AuNPs-probes for V. parahaemolyticus detection with a linear detection interval of 102-105 CFU/mL and a minimum detection concentration of 102 CFU/mL (Ren et al., 2023). Therefore, fluorescent biosensors based on aptamer demonstrates its potential to overcome the obstacles of other methods mentioned above.

Xylan, a non-aromatic polysaccharide with a backbone structure composed of β-(1→4)-linked D-xylose units, serves as a primary constituent of hemicelluloses. Its advantages, including low cost, renewability, and wide availability, position xylan as a promising candidate for carbon precursor applications. Furthermore, compared to other precursors, xylan exhibits greater sensitivity to thermo-chemical processes, enhancing its potential in the preparation process of carbon dots (Cai et al., 2022). Carbon dots (CDs), a class of graphene-like carbon materials with size < 10 nm, are remarkable fluorescent nanomaterials with superiorities of high stability, good dispersity, biocompatibility, excellent electron transfer efficiency, abundant edge defects, and tunable photoluminescent properties (Chen et al., 2023; Farshbaf et al., 2018; Keerthana et al., 2023; Malavika et al., 2022; Ou et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2023). Xylan-based carbon dots (CDs) are one of the remarkable fluorescent nanomaterials, facilitating the formation of stable and abundant cores of CDs under hydrothermal conditions. CDs boast rich functional groups like carboxyl groups, which makes it convenient for the attachment of carbon dots upon the immobilized substrate and the loading of aptamers (Chen et al., 2024; Ou et al., 2024). CDs modified with aptamer were reported for targeting the CA-125 cell surface antigen and imaging by Heidari’s team (Heidari et al., 2022). However, using CDs as the only fluorescent signal renders it susceptible to interference, which often leads to deviated results in complicated samples (Shi et al., 2022). Fortunately, ratiometric fluorescence platforms present two distinctive peaks, whose intensity ratio realize self-calibration to improve quantification accuracy and reliability. Rhodamine B (RhB) is a kind of hydrophilic xanthene dye commonly used in fluorescent sensor (Chen et al., 2022; Mishra and Gogate, 2010). However, RhB is associated with high reproductive toxicity, aggregation-caused quenching and photobleaching (Priya et al., 2024). To overcome these limitations, silica nanoparticles provide a stable environment for Rhodamine B, enhancing its light stability and protecting it against quenching and photobleaching (Meng et al., 2024). Furthermore, silica nanoparticles can serve as an excellent framework to integrate dual-signal and response system.

In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent platform functionalized with V. parahaemolyticus aptamers (RFP-Apt.D) was developed for the sensitive detection of V. parahaemolyticus The platform consists of three key components ( Scheme 1): (1) silica nanoparticles embedded with RhB dye (RhB@SiO₂) through a hydrolysis reaction, serving as an internal reference, (2) CDs emitting blue fluorescence are uniformly distributed on the surface of RhB@SiO₂, acting as the responsive element; and (3) Dabcyl-modified aptamers attached to CDs, acting as specific recognition component for target bacteria. The RhB@SiO₂ nanoparticles emit orange fluorescence for internal calibration, while CDs exhibit fluorescence intensity changes in presence of V. parahaemolyticus, enabling sensitive and accurate quantification via the ratio of blue to orange fluorescence. The aptamer’s high affinity for V. parahaemolyticus ensures its specific recognition, while the Dabcyl modification quenches fluorescence in the absence of the target, improving signal contrast. This platform combines sensitivity and specificity, making it ideal for detection of V. parahaemolyticus in complex samples.

Xylan extracted from bagasse was provided by Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Rhodamine B (RhB), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99 %), urea, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS), were gained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai China). Ammonia (NH3·H2O, 35 %), ethanol and other chemicals were of analytical grade. The fixed ssDNA and the specific V. parahaemolyticus aptamers with 4-[4-(dimethylamino)phenylazo] benzoic acid N-succinimide ester (Dabcyl) were sourced from Beijing Tsingke Biotech Co., Ltd. (Beijing China). The sequence of the fixed ssDNA was given as follows: 5’-ATT TTT AGA CGAAT TTTTT-3’ (Liu et al., 2022). The sequence of the specific V. parahaemolyticus aptamer was given as follows: 5’-ATTCG TCT AAA AAT GGG CAA AGA AAC AGT GAC TCG TTG AGA TACT-3’ (Liu et al., 2022). Vibrio parahaemolyticus (V. parahaemolyticus) (ATCC 17802), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 43300), Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922), and Salmonella typhimurium (ATCC 14028) were provided by Guangdong Microbial Culture Collection Center (GDMCC). Seawater was got from Nansha in Guangzhou. Clams were bought in local market. Sample test papers were bought by Guangdong Dayuan Oasis Food Safety Technology Co., Ltd.

Transmission electron microscopes (TEM) images were got from JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained from scanning electron microscopy (Zeiss, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were got by D8 Advance X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (Bruker, Germany) and were deconvoluted by XPSpeak41. The UV–vis absorption spectrum was investigated by a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Daojin, China). FT-IR spectra were investigated on a Tensor 27 infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction was conducted on a D8 advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Zeta potential was determined using a Malvern Zetasizer instrument (ZSU3200, Malvern, England). PL spectra were collected on Hitachi F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a Xe lamp as excitation source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of xylan derived carbon dots with blue fluorescence (BCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: BCDs were synthesized using a straightforward hydrothermal method according to previous work (Cai et al., 2022). 1 g of xylan was dissolved in 20 mL of NaOH/urea aqueous solution (8 %/12 %, w/w). Then the mixed solution was poured into a 25 mL stainless steel Teflon-liner autoclave and reacted at 240 °C for 24 h. Then the mixed solution was centrifugated at 14,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitate. Dialyzing by a dialysis bag of 500 Da molecular weight cut off (MWCO) for a week, BCDs were obtained and then stored at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ammoniated RhB doped silica (RhB@SiO2)
ce:section.ce:para: 47.9 mg of RhB and 1 mL of TEOS were dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol, and magnetically stirred for 15 minutes to prepare a RhB-silica precursor solution. Then, RhB-silica precursor solution was added dropwise to another TEOS dispersion and stirred for 30 minutes. Subsequently, 1 mL of NH3·H2O and 3 mL of deionized water were added and stirred for 6 hours. Then, 3 mL of TEOS and 4 mL of NH3·H2O were added and stirred for 18 h to attain RhB doped silica (RhB@SiO2), which was washed with deionized water and ethanol until the supernatant became transparent and nonfluorescent. Subsequently, RhB@SiO2 was redispersed in 40 mL of ethanol. 1 mL of APTES was added and magnetically stirred at room temperature for 12 h. RhB@SiO2-NH2 was obtained by centrifugation, washed with deionized water for three times and then redispersed in 5 mL of deionized water for storage. Silica nanosphere were crafted utilizing the identical synthesis protocol, albeit devoid of the RhB component.

383.4 mg of EDC and 230.2 mg of NHS were successively added to 5 mL of BCD, and magnetically stirred for 30 minutes at room temperature to activate carboxyl groups on BCD. Then, 5 mL of RhB@SiO2-NH2 were added, and the mixture was continued magnetically stirring for 2 h at room temperature. During the activating and synthesis process, the pH of the solution needed to be kept at about 5. The resulting precipitate RFP was obtained by centrifugation, washed with deionized water for three times and then redispersed in 5 mL of deionized water for storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ratiometric fluorescence platform with Dabcyl-modified aptamer (RFP-Apt.D)
ce:section.ce:para: Before the amidation coupling between RFP and fixed ssDNA, the fixed ssDNA needs to be annealed at 95 °C for 5 minutes and gradient cooled to room temperature. Then, 383 mg of EDC and 230 mg of NHS were used to activate the remaining carboxyl groups on RFP for 30 minutes. Subsequently, the amino-modified fixed ssDNA (fDNA) was added, and the mixture was magnetically stirred for 2 h at room temperature. RFP-fDNA was obtained by centrifugation, washed with deionized water for three times and then redispersed in 5 mL of deionized water.

After annealing at 95 °C for 5 minutes and gradient cooling to room temperature, specific V. parahaemolyticus aptamers were mixed with RFP- fDNA and gently magnetically stirred for 2 h to facilitate hybridization between the specific V. parahaemolyticus aptamers and the fixed ssDNAs. RFP-Apt.D was obtained by centrifugation, washed with deionized water for three times and then redispersed in 5 mL of deionized water before being stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cultivation and detection of V. parahaemolyticus
ce:section.ce:para: V. parahaemolyticus was cultivated in Tryptic Soy Polmyxin Brot Base supplemented with additional 3 % NaCl at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the cultivated bacteria were centrifuged and washed by normal saline for 3 times. Following this, they were stepwise diluted with normal saline to various concentrations. Equal volume of V. parahaemolyticus and RFP-Apt.D were mixed and incubated for 30 minutes under oscillation. Subsequently, 200 μL of the mixture was sampled for measurement of fluorescence intensity in range of 385–650 nm with excitation of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of foodborne pathogens in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Seawater and clams were chosen as real matrix to test the performance of the fluorescent platform. Seawater was centrifugated to remove precipitate. Clams (meretrix) were first minced and dispersed in ultrapure water, and then filtrated by qualitative filter papers to remove suspended solids. Seawater or extraction of clams containing V. parahaemolyticus was mixed with RFP-Apt.D and incubated for 30 minutes under oscillation. Subsequently, 200 μL of the mixture was sampled for fluorescence measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of SiO2, RhB@SiO2, RFP, RFP-Apt.D
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–visible absorption spectra of synthesized SiO2, RhB, BCDs, RhB@SiO2, RFP, and RFP-Apt.D were shown in Fig. 1a. No significant absorption was observed in the spectrum of SiO2. RhB showed a characteristic absorption peak at 551 nm, attributed to the oxygen heterocycle (Song et al., 2011), and this absorption peak also showed int the spectra of RhB@SiO2, RFP, and RFP-Apt.D.

FT-IR spectra were analyzed to identify functional groups and interactions among SiO2, BCDs, RhB@SiO2, RFP, RFP-Apt.D. As depicted in Fig. 1b, characteristic peaks at 470 cm−1, 800 cm−1, 955 cm−1, and 1100 cm−1 were observed in spectra of samples contained SiO2, ascribed to Si-O-Si bending vibration, Si-O-Si anti-symmetric stretching vibration, Si-OH stretching vibration, and Si-O-Si symmetric stretching vibration, respectively (Ellerbrock et al., 2022; Ritz and Valášková, 2018; Sarma et al., 1992). SiO2 also showed a peak at 1236 cm−1 assigned to Si-C stretching vibration, due to a small amount of residue of unreacted TEOS. The peak at the same place present in RhB@SiO2, but it is attributed to not only Si-C in TEOS, but also the aromatic skeletal C-O-C stretch in encapsulated RhB. Compared with RhB@SiO2, RhB@SiO2-NH2 exhibited an additional board peak around 3400 cm−1 and 3600 cm−1 arising from N-H stretching vibration and an enhancement in the peak at 1236 cm−1 arising from C-N stretching vibration, indicating successful amination of RhB@SiO2. The presence of carboxyl groups in BCDs is demonstrated by the C = O stretching vibration peak at 1630 cm−1. Upon the formation of amide bonds between BCDs and RhB@SiO2-NH2, the characteristic C = O stretching vibration peak was also observed in RFP, which was more obvious than that of RhB@SiO2-NH2. Following aptamer modification, a C-N tensile vibration peak stands at 1600 cm−1, attributed to amide bonds in both aptamers and Dabcyl, indicated the successful preparation of RFP-Apt.D.

The size distribution of SiO2, RhB@SiO2, RFP, and RFP-Apt.D were assessed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM). And zeta potential analysis was performed to verify their surface charge characteristics, thereby offering indirect proof to investigate each step in whole synthetic processes. The mean diameter of the SiO2 nanospheres was approximately 192 nm, whereas the RhB@SiO2 exhibited diameters of 155 nm (Fig. 1c). The disparity in size was attributed to the incorporation of RhB. In the process of TEOS hydrolysis and RhB@SiO2 formation, the doping of negatively charged RhB into SiO2 increased the internal electrostatic repulsion between TEOS and the RhB@SiO2 cores, thereby yielding a reduced size of RhB@SiO2. Meanwhile, diameter of RFP and RFP-Apt.D increased to approximately 160 nm, 164 nm, respectively (Fig. 1c). As depicted in Fig. 1d, the zeta potentials of RhB and RhB@SiO2 were −2.69 mV and −14.4 mV, respectively. After a meticulous surface amination on RhB@SiO2, the zeta potential of RhB@SiO2-NH2 ascended to an impressive 30.3 mV, indicating a significant alteration in its surface properties. The BCDs, known for their abundant carboxyl groups, showed a zeta potential of −14.3 mV. Due to the attraction between opposite charges, Blue CDs were drawn towards the surface of RhB@SiO2-NH2 and anchored through amidation reaction, which descended the zeta potential to −34.7 mV.

The XRD analysis (Fig. S1) was carried out to confirm the phase structure and crystallinity of SiO2, RhB@SiO2, RFP, and RFP-Apt.D. A broad peak at 2θ = 22°, assigned to the SiO2 nanoparticles, was observed in spectra of all samples (Liang et al., 2012). It indicated that SiO2 existed in an amorphous state (Zhang et al., 2019). Moreover, none of them exhibited any discernible crystal structure.

To obtain further insight into the componential and structural change, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed. Regarding SiO2, RhB@SiO2, RFP, and RFP-Apt.D, the only chemical structure remained unaffected through the whole synthesis process was SiO2, all with the same axisymmetric peak showing at 103.5 eV, corresponding to oxidation state of Si as Si4+ (Fig. S2) (Lin et al., 2023). The RhB, doped into the interior of the SiO2 nanoparticles via physical adsorption, did not affect the chemical states of silicon atoms. As shown in Fig. S4, the fine spectra of RhB@SiO2, RFP and RFP-Apt.D O1s possessed two peaks. The peak at 533 eV is attributed to O-C = O and C = O on the carboxyl-rich BCDs and the peak at 532 eV is corresponded to C-O-C, C-OH and Si-O (Dalby et al., 2007; Shi et al., 2019). The fine spectra of RhB@SiO2 C1s (Fig. S3) reveals characteristic peaks of C-O, and C-C at 286.81 eV and 284.71 eV respectively, indicating the presence of RhB. Comparing the fine spectra of C1s between the RhB@SiO2 and RFP, the latter one exhibits a new C = O peak at 288.39 eV attributed to the carboxyl groups on BCDs (Shi et al., 2019). As for the RFP, the peak at 286.35 eV is correspond to C-O, C-O-Si and C-N (Beshkov et al., 1999; Wallart et al., 2005). After the modification of aptamers, the C - O peak become wider and a little stronger, while C = O characteristic peak become a little weaker in the spectrum of C1s for RFP-Apt.D. It may indicate that C-N and C-O groups increased with the decoration of fDNA and Apt.D. The N1s peak in the Fig. S5 further confirms the successful attachment of BCDs on RhB@SiO2 and the successful decoration of Apt.D. Additionally, the peak at 400 eV in the fine spectra of N1s for RFP, attributed to sp2 nitrogen bonding, arises from amide linkages between BCDs and RhB@SiO2 and the pyridinic N of BCDs (Wang et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2017). The other peak at 401.82 eV attributed to the unreacted amino groups on the surface of RhB@SiO2. The N1s spectrum for RFP-Apt.D shows a wider sp2 peak, attributed to the azo bond from Dabcyl and C-N from amide linkages among BCDs and fDNA. XPS analysis of RFP-Apt.D reveals new characteristic peak of a new element P from ssDNA, confirming successful attachment of the Dabcyl-modified aptamers to RFP. In the P2p fine spectrum, the characteristic peak of P 2p3/2 is observed at 133.5–133.6 eV (Howell et al., 2011).

The morphological characteristics of SiO2, RFP, and RFP-Apt.D nanoparticles were examined via scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). As presented in Fig. 2a & b, RhB@SiO2 exhibited a consistent, spherical morphology with diameters of approximately 155 nm. The spherical BCDs showed a uniform size with an average diameter of 2.5 nm (Fig. 2c). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2d) showcased the well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, attributed to the (1120) lattice of graphene (Cai et al., 2022). Through amide reaction, BCDs were affixed onto the RhB@SiO2, leading to slightly enlarged dimension of RFP compared to RhB@SiO2 (Fig. 2f). Elemental mapping conducted via Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) corroborated the result. The presence of O and Si elements was indicative of the SiO2 component, while the N and C elements confirmed the successful conjugation of BCDs within RFP. Furthermore, the EDS spectrum of RFP-Apt.D revealed the characteristic P element, associated with the phosphodiester linkage in the oligonucleotide, which collectively underscored the successful synthesis of RFP-Apt.D.

Fig. 3a elucidated the fluorescent properties of SiO2, RhB, BCDs, RhB@SiO2, RFP, and RFP-Apt.D under excitation of 365 nm. As an optical inert material, SiO2 only possessed the Raman peak of water at 420 nm, but took role as the protector of RhB and carrier of BCD. BCD showed fluorescent emission peak at 430 nm, while that of RhB was at 575 nm. The fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of BCDs, RhB@SiO2, RFP, and RFP-Apt.D have been tested using the integral sphere method to provide additional clarity. Moreover, the QY of BCDs, RhB@SiO2, RFP, and RFP-Apt.D were determined to be 23.04 %, 22.49 %, 25.74 %, and 21.64 %, respectively. Through doping of RhB, the RhB@SiO2 also showcased a peak at 575 nm with a little weaker intensity than RhB, indicating that the RhB doping went through a physical adsorption process and maintained the structure of RhB unchanged. Once BCDs attached on RhB@SiO2, the obtained RFP presented blue fluorescent peak at 430 nm in addition to orange fluorescent peak at 575 nm. Due to the quenching of BCDs and RhB by V. parahaemolyticus aptamer with Dabcyl, the peak referred to blue fluorescence (430 nm) and orange fluorescence (575 nm) weaken in the fluorescent spectra of RFP-Apt.D.

The excitation dependent photoluminescence of RFP is shown in Fig. 3b. With the increase of excitation wavelength from 340 nm to 380 nm, the blue-fluorescent emission from BCDs gradually shifts from 410 nm to 435 nm, while the orange fluorescent emission from silica-coated RhB stands the same position of 575 nm. The difference on the emission shift between them is ascribed to their structural uniformity. The BCDs is consisted by various carbon dots with similar fluorescent center and degree of distribution, compared to RhB with defined molecular formula. By selecting appropriate excitation wavelengths, the emission wavelength can be tailored to facilitate the Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) process (Kim et al., 2015). For optimal FRET efficiency, an excitation wavelength of 360 nm was employed. As shown in Fig. S7, this choice was based on the significant spectral overlap between the fluorescence emission of BCDs upon 360 nm excitation and the absorption band of Apt.D, which is a critical requirement for effective energy transfer.

As shown in Fig. 3c, the CIE coordinates of the BCDs and RhB@SiO2 were determined to be (0.16, 0.12) and (0.40, 0.37), respectively. After grafting BCDs to RhB@SiO2, the CIE coordinate of RFP moved to (0.21, 0.19). Then, due to the modification of aptamer, the CIE coordinate of RFP-Apt.D shifted to (0.22, 0.21). As displayed in the inset of Fig. 3c, under UV irradiation, RhB@SiO2 showed orange fluorescence from RhB, and BCDs showed blue fluorescence. RFP displayed light pink fluorescence for the simultaneous existence of RhB and BCDs. Upon adding the V. parahaemolyticus aptamers with Dabcyl, the blue fluorescence and the orange fluorescence were both quenched and the blue fluorescence quenched more, so the fluorescent color of the platform turned a little back to orange.

The fluorescence lifetime of RFP, RFP-Apt.D and was measured, as shown in Fig. 3d and Table 1. It is calculated to be 2.62 ns by fitting the fluorescence lifetime curve of RFP (430 nm). However, the fluorescence lifetime of RFP-Apt.D (430 m) was fitted and calculated to be 2.05 ns (Pan et al., 2024). The fluorescence lifetime shortening is likely caused by FRET between Dabcyl and BCDs. In this system, BCDs acts as the excited-state donor and Dabcyl as the acceptor. The coordination between Apt.D and fDNA position BCDs and Dabcyl in close proximity, allowing Dabcyl to absorb energy from excited BCDs. This energy transfer reduces the fluorescence lifetime of BCDs. Based on the established equation for evaluating FRET efficiency, E = 1 −τD–A/τDE, where E represents the energy transfer efficiency, and τD and τD–A denote the fluorescence lifetimes of the donor and the donor–acceptor pair, respectively. The energy transfer efficiency of the RFP-Apt.D composite was determined to be 22.05 % (Wang et al., 2015). Upon the presence of V. parahaemolyticus, Apt.D detaches from the surface of RFP due to its strong binding affinity to V. parahaemolyticus, increasing the distance between BCDs and Dabcyl. So, the FRET is halted and fluorescence lifetime recovers to 2.38 ns. The orange fluorescence lifetimes of RFP, RFP-Apt.D, and RFP-Apt.D-V. parahaemolyticus were compared. No significant difference was observed between RFP-Apt.D and RFP-Apt.D-V. parahaemolyticus. RFP-Apt.D exhibited shorter lifetimes compared to RFP, while adding V. parahaemolyticus led to recovering parts of the lifetime. This observation indicates that RhB near the surface of silicon spheres was quenched by Dabcyl due to FRET. It is consistent with the phenomenon that the fluorescence lifetime at 430 nm cannot be fully restored either.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity, selectivity and stability of RFP-Apt.D
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4a, the spherical structure on the left, representing the RFP-Apt.D, illustrates the quenching of blue fluorescence caused by FRET between the BCDs and Dabcyl. Upon exposure to V. parahaemolyticus, the specific aptamers selectively recognized and tightly bound to the bacterial targets, leading to their dissociation from the probe. Consequently, the spherical structure on the right shows that Apt.D detached from RFP-Apt.D after successfully identifying the bacteria, resulting in the restoration of the blue fluorescence of the carbon dots. This strategy enabled the ratiometric fluorescent platform to achieve highly sensitive detection of V. parahaemolyticus with a rapid response time of only 30 minutes.

The performance of this ratiometric fluorescent platform (RFP-Apt.D) for detection of V. parahaemolyticus was investigated. It is observable that both blue fluorescent peak from BCDs and orange fluorescent peak from RhB were influenced by the addition of V. parahaemolyticus, showing a linear relationship between ratio of fluorescence intensity (I430/I575) and concentration of V. parahaemolyticus in a wide linear response range of 8–8 × 105 CFU/mL, the calibration curve shows regression equation of y = 0.03133x + 2.3745 with an R2 of 0.98614. In this equation, y represents the fluorescent ratio, and x represents the concentration of V. parahaemolyticus Furthermore, this ratiometric fluorescent platform possesses a limit of detection of 1.15 CFU/mL. Superior to most reported methods for the detection of V. parahaemolyticus ( Table 2 and Fig. 4g).

To assess the storage stability of RFP-Apt.D, its fluorescence intensity was monitored over 7 days at 4°C, with results presented in Fig. 4f. The fluorescence intensity showed minimal variation during this period, demonstrating excellent stability. Additionally, after 7 days of storage at 4°C, RFP-Apt.D retained over 88 % capture efficiency for V. parahaemolyticus, further confirming its robust performance stability (Fig. S6).

To assess the selectivity of the developed ratio fluorescent method for V. parahaemolyticus, four prevalent pathogenic microorganisms, including E. coli, S. aureus, Salmonella, and P. aeruginosa were investigated. The quotient obtained by dividing the blue responsive fluorescence intensity by the initial blue fluorescence intensity was designated as the indicator of specificity. Each of the aforementioned bacterial strains was evaluated at a concentration of 105 CFU/mL, with a solution of V. parahaemolyticus at an equivalent concentration serving as a positive control. The ratio of recovered fluorescence intensity to its original intensity was acknowledged as the factor for evaluating specificity. As shown in Fig. 4d, only V. parahaemolyticus generated high level of fluorescence recovery, while the other bacteria species are nearly impossible to restore the quenched fluorescence. It proved that the ratiometric fluorescent detection method was highly specific for V. parahaemolyticus.

To validate the applicability and practicability of the RFP-Apt.D ratiometric fluorescence platform, this approach was applied to actual sample analysis, adhering to standardized assay protocols. Detection and recovery trials were executed by introducing V. parahaemolyticus at varying concentrations into seawater and clam samples. The corresponding V. parahaemolyticus concentration was demonstrated through parallel experiments using the plate coating method ( Fig. 5a). As illustrated in Table 3 and Fig. 5b & c, when added concentrations were only 16 CFU/mL, there is a significant deviation in the recovery rate of 0.809 in seawater samples and 1.202 in clam samples, and RSD values of 0.283 in seawater samples and 0.276 in clam samples. When the added concentrations increased to 1.6 × 103 CFU/mL, the recovery rates spanned to 0.986 (seawater) and 0.927 (clam), accompanied by relative standard deviation (RSD) changed to 0.138 (seawater) and 0.163 (clam). When the added concentration further increases, the recovery rate becomes closer to 1, with the one in seawater samples reaching 1.020 and the one in clam samples reaching 1.030, and the RSD was 0.228 (seawater) and 0.169 (clam). Despite encountering broader fluctuations in recovery at the low added concentrations of 16 CFU/mL, the ratiometric fluorescence platform undeniably showcased promising prospects for the accurate detection of aquatic specimens.

In summary, RFP-Apt.D with ratiometric fluorescence was developed for detection of V. parahaemolyticus. RFP-Apt.D was assembled by RhB loaded SiO2 as an internal reference, and carbon dots as the responding signal, followed by immobilizing Dabcyl-modified aptamers for specific recognition. Notably, the RFP-Apt.D showed short responding time of 30 minutes and a low limit of detection of 1.15 CFU/mL (S/N = 3) with wide linear range of 8–8 × 105 CFU/mL. Moreover, the RFP-Apt.D was successfully utilized to detect V. parahaemolyticus in real samples, including seawater and clams, with increased accuracy and good reliability through easy pretreatment without enrichment. This work offers a universal approach for the development of ratiometric fluorescent sensor tailored for bacterial detection.

Feng Xiwen: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yu Shanshan: Validation, Supervision. Cai Jihai: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Wang Xiaoying: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No. 2024A1515011818), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22208114), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2022M721195).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2025.120871.
